The selloff reflected a broader reassessment of geopolitical risk, with market participants weighing the possibility that the conflict could expand beyond its current theater and draw in additional state and non-state actors. Defense and energy stocks bucked the downward trend in some markets, while airlines, shipping companies, and consumer discretionary sectors bore the brunt of the selling pressure.

The escalation comes at a particularly sensitive moment for global markets, which have already been contending with uncertainty around central bank policy paths, persistent inflation in parts of the developed world, and slowing growth in key emerging economies. Analysts warn that a sustained disruption to Middle Eastern energy flows could push oil prices significantly higher, complicating the inflation fight for the Federal Reserve and other major central banks and potentially delaying anticipated rate cuts.

Market strategists are urging caution in the near term, noting that geopolitical conflicts of this nature tend to inject prolonged volatility rather than one-time shocks. The situation in the Middle East remains fluid, and the trajectory of both the conflict and its economic consequences will depend heavily on whether diplomatic efforts can contain the fallout before it engulfs a broader swath of the region's energy infrastructure.

articles.headline: Strait of Hormuz Faces Months-Long Closure as Iran Conflict Escalates
articles.subheadline: Bloomberg's Authers warns prolonged shutdown of world's most critical oil chokepoint will ripple through global energy markets
articles.body:
The Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply flows — appears headed for a closure lasting months, according to Bloomberg Opinion columnist John Authers, who painted a stark picture of the unfolding crisis from 40,000 feet above the conflict zone on March 12.

Writing from a commercial flight rerouted to skirt Iranian airspace now designated a war zone, Authers described the potential ramifications of the closure as "too hard to count." The strait, which separates Iran from the Arabian Peninsula at a width of just 21 miles at its narrowest point, has long been considered global oil's most vulnerable chokepoint. A prolonged shutdown would choke off tanker traffic carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Qatar to markets across Asia, Europe, and beyond.

The conflict driving the closure involves what Authers characterized as three of the most contentious governments on the planet — the United States, Iran, and Israel — each carrying distinct political agendas into the escalation. While the full scope of military operations remains fluid, the rerouting of civilian air traffic away from Iranian airspace signals a severity that markets and governments will be forced to reckon with in the weeks ahead.

The economic implications of even a short-term closure would be severe. Energy analysts have long modeled Hormuz disruption scenarios, with estimates suggesting that a sustained blockage could remove upward of 17 million barrels per day from global supply — a volume no combination of strategic petroleum reserves and alternative pipeline routes could fully offset. A months-long timeline, as Authers suggests, would likely trigger a dramatic repricing of crude oil, cascading effects on inflation worldwide, and potential rationing in import-dependent economies across Asia.

For markets already navigating uncertainty around global growth and monetary policy, the Hormuz closure introduces a supply shock of historic proportions. Traders, central bankers, and heads of state now face a question with no easy answer: how to sustain the global economy when its most critical energy artery is severed for an extended period.

articles.headline: Notre Dame's St. Benedict Ion Trap Passes Key Commissioning Milestone
articles.subheadline: Radio frequency quadrupole ion guide achieves over 95% transport efficiency, advancing a precision test of the Standard Model
articles.body:
A critical component of the Superallowed Transition Beta-Neutrino Decay Ion Coincidence Trap — known as St. Benedict — has cleared its first major commissioning test at the University of Notre Dame, bringing physicists one step closer to a precision probe of the electroweak sector of the Standard Model.

Researchers led by Regan Zite and Maxime Brodeur at Notre Dame's Nuclear Science Laboratory reported that the radio frequency quadrupole (RFQ) ion guide, a key beam-manipulation element in the St. Benedict apparatus, achieved a transport efficiency exceeding 95 percent for ions delivered from an upstream RF carpet chamber. A secondary off-line ion source, mounted at 90 degrees to the main beam path for calibration purposes, yielded a lower but still functional efficiency of roughly 60 percent. The results, detailed in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server on March 5, represent the first comprehensive off-line performance benchmarks for the device.

St. Benedict is designed to measure the beta-neutrino angular correlation parameter in superallowed mixed mirror beta decay transitions — a class of nuclear decays that provides a uniquely sensitive window into the unitarity of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) quark-mixing matrix. Any deviation from unitarity in the CKM matrix would signal physics beyond the Standard Model, making high-precision measurements of this kind a priority for the nuclear and particle physics communities. The ion trap approach requires exquisite control over radioactive ion beams, and the RFQ ion guide is responsible for efficiently transporting and shaping those beams before they reach the trapping region.

The 95 percent efficiency figure is a strong result for this stage of development, suggesting that the ion guide will not be a limiting factor in the experiment's overall sensitivity. The lower performance of the 90-degree off-line source is expected given the geometric constraints of injecting ions perpendicular to the main beam axis, and the team indicated that this source is intended primarily for testing and calibration rather than production running.

St. Benedict remains under construction at the Nuclear Science Laboratory, with the full system expected to integrate multiple beam-manipulation stages before taking data with radioactive isotopes. The successful commissioning of the RFQ ion guide marks a tangible step forward for an experiment that could ultimately sharpen one of the most demanding consistency checks in fundamental physics.

articles.headline: SEC and CFTC Sign Historic Pact to Unify Crypto Oversight
articles.subheadline: Memorandum of understanding ends decades of regulatory turf wars, with coordinated crypto framework as a top priority
articles.body:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission have signed a memorandum of understanding that formalizes joint oversight of digital assets, marking the most significant step yet toward resolving the jurisdictional ambiguity that has plagued the American crypto industry for years.

The agreement, released Wednesday, commits the two agencies to sharing resources and data, coordinating enforcement actions, aligning regulatory definitions, and — most notably for the digital asset sector — building what the document calls a "fit-for-purpose regulatory framework for crypto assets and other emerging technologies." Under the new arrangement, regulated firms will be able to request combined meetings with both agencies simultaneously to discuss policy questions and product applications, a sharp departure from the historically siloed and sometimes contradictory guidance that characterized the previous regulatory regime.

SEC Chairman Paul Atkins framed the pact as long overdue. "For decades, regulatory turf wars, duplicative agency registrations, and different sets of regulations between the SEC and CFTC have stifled innovation and pushed market participants to other jurisdictions," Atkins said in a statement. The agreement extends to enforcement as well: where both agencies have jurisdiction over a case, their staffs will now confer on potential charges, litigation strategy, and public communications — addressing a longstanding complaint from crypto firms that found themselves facing parallel and sometimes conflicting accusations from both regulators.

The political backdrop makes the deal's passage essentially frictionless. Both agencies are led by appointees of President Donald Trump, who has embraced the crypto industry since taking office. The CFTC is currently run by sole Republican Chairman Mike Selig on an otherwise empty five-member commission, while Atkins leads a three-Republican SEC with both Democratic seats vacant. Both chairmen previously worked with crypto clients before assuming their government roles.

The memorandum arrives at a moment of broader regulatory recalibration in Washington. Congress is separately advancing stablecoin legislation, and major financial institutions like Wells Fargo are signaling deeper crypto engagement. For an industry that has long cited regulatory uncertainty as its chief obstacle in the United States, the SEC-CFTC accord represents perhaps the clearest signal yet that the era of interagency conflict over digital assets is ending — replaced by a coordinated framework whose precise contours remain to be defined.

articles.headline: Oil Surges Past $100 as Record IEA Reserve Release Fails to Calm Markets
articles.subheadline: All 32 IEA members pledge 400 million barrels, but Iran's threat to target ships in Strait of Hormuz keeps prices climbing
articles.body:
Crude oil prices blew past the $100-per-barrel threshold on Thursday even as the International Energy Agency announced the largest coordinated release of emergency reserves in its history, underscoring the depth of market anxiety over the escalating conflict with Iran.

Brent crude surged nearly 9% during Asian trading to top $100 a barrel, shrugging off a pledge by all 32 IEA member nations to collectively release 400 million barrels of oil from strategic stockpiles. The release more than doubles the previous record set in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Yet the move — described by Martin Ma of the Singapore Institute of Technology as "historically significant" but only a "temporary buffer" — has done little to reassure traders bracing for prolonged supply disruption in the Middle East.

The immediate catalyst for the price spike is Iran's escalating campaign in the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's energy supplies transit. On Wednesday, a spokesperson for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps warned that any vessel linked to the United States, Israel, or their allies would be targeted, and predicted oil could reach $200 per barrel. "You will not be able to artificially lower the price of oil," the spokesperson said, framing regional energy flows as inseparable from the broader military conflict. Global oil markets have been in turmoil since the U.S. and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran on February 28, with Brent briefly touching nearly $120 earlier this week.

The fallout is rippling through consumer economies worldwide. In the United States, the average price of gasoline climbed above $3.50 per gallon as of Tuesday, according to the American Automobile Association. But the pain is most acute across Asia, where heavy dependence on Middle Eastern oil has triggered fuel-hoarding behavior and emergency government measures. Long queues formed at gas stations in the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam, while Thai authorities ordered most government workers to stay home to conserve energy and restricted non-essential overseas travel. The Philippines went further, implementing a four-day government work week to reduce fuel consumption.

The disconnect between the record reserve release and the market's upward trajectory illustrates a fundamental problem: strategic stockpiles can bridge short-term gaps, but they cannot substitute for the steady flow of tanker traffic through the world's most critical chokepoint. As long as Iran's threats to shipping remain credible, analysts expect oil prices to stay elevated — with the $200 figure Tehran cited no longer dismissed as mere rhetoric.

articles.headline: Bonk.fun Domain Hijacked, Crypto Wallet Drainer Installed
articles.subheadline: Hackers seized a team account at the Solana token launchpad, planting malicious code that targeted users who signed a fake message
articles.body:
Hackers compromised the domain of Bonk.fun, a community-driven Solana token issuance platform backed by Raydium and the BONK token, installing a cryptocurrency wallet drainer that targeted visitors to the site. The platform's operator warned users on Thursday to avoid the website until further notice.

The operator, who goes by the name Tom and posts under the handle @SolportTom on X, disclosed that attackers hijacked a team account to gain control of the domain. Users who connected their wallets and signed what appeared to be a terms-of-service message on the compromised site were exposed to the drainer, which is designed to siphon funds from connected wallets. Bonk's official X account corroborated the warning.

Tom emphasized that the breach was detected quickly and that its scope appears limited. Users who had previously connected to Bonk.fun before the attack were not affected, nor were trades executed through third-party terminals. Only those who interacted with the fraudulent prompt after the hijacking were at risk. The exact dollar value of losses has not been disclosed, though rapid community alerts seem to have contained the damage. "We're doing everything in our power to fix the situation," Tom said, noting the platform's eight-month track record with its user base.

The incident highlights a persistent and underappreciated vulnerability in decentralized finance: frontend attacks. Even as crypto ecosystems attract growing institutional participation and sophisticated on-chain security audits, the web interfaces through which most users access protocols remain conventional attack surfaces susceptible to domain hijacking, DNS spoofing, and social engineering. Unlike smart contract exploits, frontend compromises can be executed without breaching underlying blockchain infrastructure, making them a favored vector for attackers seeking quick payoffs.

BONK, the Solana-based meme token that underpins the platform, was trading at roughly $0.000008 at the time of the breach. Solana itself was priced near $84.92. The broader crypto market showed muted reaction, with Bitcoin holding above $69,400 and Ethereum trading around $2,025. The Bonk.fun team has not yet provided a timeline for restoring the domain or detailed its remediation steps.

articles.headline: New Analytic Model Maps Magnetic Field Behavior in Fusion Implosions
articles.subheadline: Researchers derive a framework for rapidly evaluating compressed magnetic field geometry in laser-driven inertial confinement fusion
articles.body:
A team of physicists has developed an analytic model that predicts how externally applied magnetic fields behave when compressed during inertial confinement fusion (ICF) implosions — a finding that could help optimize one of the most promising strategies for boosting fusion energy yields.

The research, led by Robert Spiers and collaborators including scientists from Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, addresses a key challenge in magnetized ICF: understanding the topology of magnetic fields as they are squeezed along with plasma during spherical implosions. When an external magnetic field is applied to an ICF capsule, the field compresses alongside the fuel, creating a magnetized hotspot that suppresses thermal energy losses and traps alpha particles — both critical for sustaining fusion reactions. However, the orientation and structure of the compressed field profoundly influence performance, making accurate prediction of field geometry essential.

The team's model reveals a nuanced picture of what happens inside the compressed hotspot. Ablation of fuel material into the hotspot amplifies the central magnetic field, but near the hotspot edge, the ablated ice produces a decaying, radially bent field with a sharp discontinuity in direction. This radial bending effectively neutralizes the field's ability to insulate the hotspot edge thermally, regardless of the initial applied field strength. In contrast, thermal insulation in the hotspot core remains strongly dependent on the strength of the applied field — a distinction that has significant implications for how experimentalists design magnetized fusion targets.

Perhaps most consequentially, the researchers applied their model to non-axial initial field configurations and found that an initially applied mirror field geometry provides the greatest suppression of thermal losses, outperforming the standard axial field configuration that has been the default in most magnetized ICF experiments to date. This result suggests that future experiments at facilities like the National Ignition Facility could achieve higher yields by exploring alternative magnetic field topologies.

The paper, submitted to Physics of Plasmas and posted to the arXiv preprint server on March 9, offers what the authors describe as a "simple, readily applicable" framework — a tool that enables rapid evaluation without resource-intensive simulations. As the fusion community works to build on the National Ignition Facility's landmark 2022 ignition achievement, techniques for enhancing yield through applied magnetic fields represent a significant avenue for progress. The new model gives researchers a faster way to explore a design space that, until now, required computationally expensive magnetohydrodynamic simulations to navigate.

articles.headline: Netflix Reportedly Pays Up to $600M for Ben Affleck's AI Firm
articles.subheadline: The acquisition of post-production startup InterPositive would rank among Netflix's largest deals ever
articles.body:
Netflix's acquisition of InterPositive, the artificial intelligence startup co-founded by actor and filmmaker Ben Affleck, may be worth as much as $600 million, according to a Bloomberg report — a figure that would make it one of the streaming giant's most expensive purchases in its history.

The deal, announced last week, brings under Netflix's roof a company that builds AI-powered tools designed to streamline the post-production process for filmmakers. InterPositive's technology assists with tasks such as identifying and resolving continuity errors and enhancing scenes, but the company has been careful to distinguish its approach from more controversial generative AI applications: it does not create new content from scratch or use footage without permission. Bloomberg's sources indicated that the upfront cash payment may be lower than the headline figure, with InterPositive's owners eligible for additional payouts contingent on hitting specific performance milestones.

If the deal reaches its full value, it would trail only Netflix's approximately $700 million acquisition of the Roald Dahl Story Company as the largest in the streamer's history. Netflix has not publicly confirmed the financial terms. The purchase signals an acceleration of the company's strategy to weave AI capabilities directly into its content pipeline. Netflix has already deployed generative AI in original productions, including a building-collapse sequence in the Argentine series "The Eternaut."

The move places Netflix squarely in an industry-wide race to harness AI for film and television production. Amazon has been assembling internal AI teams dedicated to its entertainment division, while Disney recently entered a partnership with OpenAI. The competitive dynamics suggest that major studios and streamers increasingly view proprietary AI tooling as a strategic differentiator in controlling costs and accelerating production timelines.

The trend has not gone unchallenged. Workers across the entertainment industry continue to voice concerns about AI's potential to displace jobs and about whether creators are being fairly compensated when their work is used to train machine learning models. Those tensions, which helped fuel the 2023 Hollywood strikes, remain unresolved even as studios press ahead with adoption. For Netflix, the InterPositive acquisition represents a bet that AI-assisted filmmaking tools can deliver meaningful efficiency gains — while navigating the fraught politics of automation in a creative industry.

articles.headline: Trump Administration Launches Trade Probes Targeting China, EU
articles.subheadline: New investigations lay groundwork for fresh tariffs after Supreme Court struck down earlier levies
articles.body:
The Trump administration has initiated a series of broad trade investigations targeting China and the European Union, signaling a renewed push to impose tariffs after the U.S. Supreme Court invalidated earlier levies that had served as a cornerstone of the president's trade agenda.

The probes, announced on March 11, represent the opening salvo in what officials have indicated will be multiple investigations designed to build a legal foundation for new import duties. The move comes as the administration seeks an alternative path to the tariff regime that the nation's highest court deemed unlawful, effectively forcing the White House to reconstruct its trade policy architecture from the ground up.

The investigations are expected to focus on trade practices by both China and the EU that the administration considers unfair or injurious to American industry. By launching formal probes — likely under established statutory authorities such as Section 301 or Section 232 — the administration aims to create a more legally durable basis for imposing tariffs than the mechanisms previously struck down by the Supreme Court. The approach suggests the White House has absorbed the judicial setback and is attempting to tariff-proof its next round of trade actions.

For global markets, the announcement introduces a fresh wave of uncertainty. The original Trump tariffs roiled supply chains, raised costs for American manufacturers and consumers, and triggered retaliatory measures from trading partners. A revival of that agenda — even through a more deliberate legal process — is likely to weigh on trade-sensitive sectors and could complicate diplomatic relationships that had only recently begun to stabilize following the court's intervention.

The EU and China have not yet formally responded to the new probes, but both trading blocs have historically signaled willingness to retaliate against what they view as protectionist U.S. measures. The investigations could take months to conclude, but their mere initiation sends a clear message: the administration views tariffs as indispensable to its economic strategy and intends to pursue them regardless of the legal obstacles encountered so far.

articles.headline: Starmer Received Epstein Warning Before Appointing Mandelson as US Envoy
articles.subheadline: Newly released government files reveal due diligence flagged 'reputational risk' nine days before ambassador was confirmed
articles.body:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer was explicitly warned that Lord Peter Mandelson's relationship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein posed a "reputational risk" before confirming him as the United Kingdom's ambassador to the United States, according to 147 pages of government documents released on Tuesday.

The files, published after Conservative MPs forced disclosure through a parliamentary humble address procedure, show that a due diligence briefing sent to Starmer on December 11, 2024 — nine days before Mandelson's appointment was confirmed — flagged a 2019 JP Morgan report finding that Epstein appeared to "maintain a particularly close relationship" with the Labour grandee. The briefing noted that Mandelson reportedly stayed at Epstein's home while the financier was incarcerated in June 2009, following his 2008 conviction for procuring an underage girl. Starmer has maintained he did not grasp the full extent of the relationship at the time of appointment.

The documents also reveal that Jonathan Powell, the prime minister's national security adviser, found the appointment process "weirdly rushed" and raised concerns "about the individual and reputation" with Starmer's then-chief of staff Morgan McSweeney. Philip Barton, the Foreign Office's most senior civil servant at the time, reportedly also held reservations. Darren Jones, the prime minister's chief secretary, acknowledged to Parliament that the due diligence process "fell short" and that steps had been taken to address "weaknesses in the system."

A separate controversy emerged around Mandelson's severance terms following his sacking last September, after new revelations about the depth of his Epstein ties surfaced. Treasury documents indicate Mandelson initially explored whether he could receive a payment equal to the remainder of his four-year salary — some £547,201 — though the BBC reports he disputes this characterization. The government ultimately settled on £75,000, with Jones telling MPs the figure was agreed to avoid costlier legal proceedings, calling Mandelson's initial position "inappropriate and unacceptable." Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch said the documents showed Starmer "has not been honest with the country," while the Liberal Democrats called on Mandelson to donate the payment to charity.

Key exchanges between Downing Street and Mandelson about his Epstein contacts remain unpublished at the Metropolitan Police's request, to avoid prejudicing an ongoing criminal investigation. Mandelson was arrested earlier this year on suspicion of misconduct in public office over allegations he passed market-sensitive government information to Epstein while serving as a minister, though his bail conditions were lifted last week. He has denied acting criminally or for personal gain, resigned from the Labour Party in February, and maintains he answered all vetting questions about Epstein truthfully. Further batches of documents are expected in coming weeks.

articles.headline: U.S. to Tap 172 Million Barrels From Strategic Reserve in IEA Plan
articles.subheadline: Trump administration joins coordinated international effort to cool surging oil prices amid Iran conflict
articles.body:
The Trump administration will release 172 million barrels of oil from the nation's Strategic Petroleum Reserve as part of a sweeping international effort coordinated by the International Energy Agency to tamp down crude and fuel prices that have surged since the outbreak of war with Iran less than two weeks ago.

The massive drawdown — one of the largest coordinated reserve releases in history — underscores the severity of the supply disruption triggered by the conflict. Global oil markets have been roiled since hostilities began in late February, with prices spiking as traders priced in the risk of prolonged disruption to Middle Eastern crude flows. Iran, an OPEC member, was producing roughly 3 million barrels per day before the war began, and the loss of even a fraction of that output has sent shockwaves through energy markets worldwide.

The U.S. contribution of 172 million barrels represents the centerpiece of the IEA's multilateral relief plan, which calls on member nations to collectively open their emergency stockpiles to flood the market with additional supply. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve, housed in underground salt caverns along the Gulf Coast, held approximately 400 million barrels prior to the announcement, though that figure has fluctuated in recent years due to prior releases and partial refills. A drawdown of this magnitude would reduce the reserve to levels not seen in decades.

The coordinated action echoes previous IEA-led interventions, including the 2022 release of 180 million barrels following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and a smaller 60-million-barrel release during Libya's civil war in 2011. However, the pace and scale of the current effort — mobilized in under two weeks — reflects the acute urgency of the crisis and the administration's desire to shield American consumers from spiraling gasoline prices.

Energy analysts cautioned that while strategic reserve releases can provide short-term relief, they are not a substitute for restoring lost production capacity. The duration of the Iran conflict and its impact on regional oil infrastructure will ultimately determine whether the intervention succeeds in stabilizing markets or merely buys time. Crude futures remained elevated in early trading following the announcement, suggesting traders are waiting for details on the timeline and logistics of the release.

articles.headline: Morgan Stanley Caps Redemptions on Private Credit Fund
articles.subheadline: The bank returned less than half the capital investors sought to withdraw, raising fresh liquidity concerns in private credit
articles.body:
Morgan Stanley has restricted investor withdrawals from one of its private credit funds, fulfilling less than half of the redemption requests submitted by clients seeking to exit the vehicle, Bloomberg reported on Tuesday.

The move highlights a persistent tension at the heart of the fast-growing private credit industry: funds that invest in illiquid, hard-to-sell loans often promise investors periodic redemption windows, but those promises can strain when a wave of withdrawal requests arrives simultaneously. By gating redemptions — a mechanism that limits how much capital can leave during any single period — Morgan Stanley is prioritizing the fund's stability over individual investors' desire for liquidity.

Private credit has ballooned into a multi-trillion-dollar asset class in recent years, attracting capital from institutional investors, wealth managers, and increasingly retail clients drawn by yields that typically exceed those available in public bond markets. Wall Street's largest banks have aggressively launched and marketed such products, positioning them as alternatives to traditional fixed income. But the sector's rapid expansion has prompted regulators and market observers to question whether investors fully appreciate the liquidity risks embedded in these vehicles, particularly during periods of market stress.

Morgan Stanley's decision to cap withdrawals is not without precedent. Similar gating events have occurred across the alternative investment landscape, most notably when Blackstone limited redemptions on its flagship real estate income trust in late 2022 and into 2023. Those episodes sparked broad debate about the suitability of semi-liquid fund structures for assets that cannot be quickly sold at fair value. The fact that a major bank is now applying the same mechanism to a private credit product suggests that liquidity mismatches in the space may be more widespread than proponents have acknowledged.

The specific fund involved, the size of the redemption shortfall, and the reasons behind the surge in withdrawal requests were not immediately detailed. Morgan Stanley has not publicly commented on the matter. Still, the episode is likely to draw scrutiny from investors and regulators alike at a moment when private credit is being positioned as a cornerstone of diversified portfolios across the wealth management industry.

articles.headline: Bullish Overtakes Coinbase as Third-Largest Crypto Exchange by Spot Volume
articles.subheadline: The institutional-focused platform saw spot trading surge 62% to $76 billion in February even as overall exchange activity declined.
articles.body:
Bullish, the institutional-only cryptocurrency exchange that trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker BLSH, has vaulted past Coinbase to claim the third-largest position among centralized crypto exchanges by spot trading volume — a milestone that underscores shifting competitive dynamics in digital asset markets.

The platform recorded $76 billion in spot trading volume during February, a 62.6% increase from the prior month and its strongest showing since October 2025, according to CoinDesk Data's February Exchange Review. That performance lifted Bullish's market share to 5.06%, eclipsing Coinbase's 4.59% share during the same period. Bullish, which is the parent company of CoinDesk, went public on the NYSE last year and has been steadily expanding its institutional trading operations.

The ascent is all the more notable given the broader market backdrop. Combined spot and derivatives trading volumes across centralized exchanges fell 2.41% in February to $5.61 trillion — the lowest level since October 2024. Spot trading specifically declined 3.01% to $1.50 trillion, weighed down by a stretch of subdued bitcoin volatility. Despite sharp moves in the first and final weeks of February, bitcoin spent much of the month oscillating in a relatively tight band between $60,000 and $70,000, sapping the speculative energy that typically fuels higher turnover.

Binance retained its position as the dominant exchange with roughly $331 billion in spot volume and approximately 22% market share, but that figure represented its lowest monthly dominance level since October 2020. The decline signals a broader redistribution of trading activity across competing platforms, as exchanges ramp up efforts to differentiate through deeper liquidity, trading incentives, tokenized securities partnerships with traditional stock exchanges, and new product categories like prediction markets.

Derivatives continued to dwarf spot activity, accounting for 73.2% of all centralized exchange volume at $4.11 trillion for the month. The data paints a picture of a maturing market where institutional platforms like Bullish are carving out significant share even during periods of reduced overall activity — a trend that could accelerate if competition continues to erode the concentration that has long characterized crypto trading.

articles.headline: Lovable Hits $400M ARR With Just 146 Employees, Defying Scale Norms
articles.subheadline: The Stockholm-based vibe-coding startup added $100 million in monthly revenue, reaching $2.77M ARR per employee
articles.body:
Lovable, the Swedish startup that lets users build apps and websites through natural language prompts, reached $400 million in annual recurring revenue in February — adding $100 million in a single month with a workforce of just 146 people. The milestone underscores the extraordinary capital efficiency of AI-native software companies and the surging demand for so-called vibe-coding tools.

The three-year-old company's growth trajectory has been remarkable even by startup standards. Lovable reported $100 million ARR last July, doubled to $200 million by November, hit $300 million in January, and now $400 million in February — a clear acceleration. At $2.77 million in ARR per employee, the company has already exceeded Gartner's prediction that a new wave of unicorns reaching $2 million ARR per employee would emerge by 2030. Lovable declined to confirm whether it still projects reaching $1 billion ARR by year's end, telling reporters only that its focus is on "helping builders scale their impact."

The company, valued at $6.6 billion, has attracted roughly 8 million users and claims that more than half of Fortune 500 companies now use its platform. Enterprise clients including Klarna and HubSpot represent a growing share of Lovable's business, as the company builds out security and enterprise-grade features to move beyond prototyping use cases and reduce churn. A recent International Women's Day promotion saw over 500,000 projects built or updated in a single day — more than double the typical daily average of approximately 200,000.

Lovable operates in an increasingly crowded field alongside Cursor, Mercor, and other AI-powered development tools. Major AI labs Anthropic and OpenAI, whose underlying models Lovable relies on, could eventually move to compete directly in the vibe-coding space through products like Claude Code and Codex. CEO and co-founder Anton Osika has publicly dismissed such concerns, and the company's accelerating revenue numbers lend weight to his confidence — at least for now.

The company plans to roughly double its headcount, with 70 open positions across Stockholm, Boston, London, New York, San Francisco, and remote roles. Its newly inaugurated Stockholm headquarters can accommodate 300 people. Even at full capacity, Lovable's revenue-per-employee ratio would remain far above industry benchmarks, raising a broader question about the future shape of technology companies built on top of generative AI infrastructure. If AI-native startups can generate hundreds of millions in revenue with skeleton crews, the implications for employment across the software industry could be profound.

articles.headline: Salesforce's $25 Billion Bond Sale Meets Tepid Demand Amid AI Fears
articles.subheadline: The CRM giant's massive debt-funded stock buyback drew just 1.4x oversubscription as investors question software firms' AI exposure
articles.body:
Salesforce Inc. encountered unusually weak investor appetite for its $25 billion bond offering on Wednesday, as concerns about the company's debt-fueled share repurchase strategy and the broader software industry's vulnerability to artificial intelligence disruption weighed on demand.

The eight-part investment-grade deal attracted a final order book of roughly $36 billion, according to people familiar with the transaction — representing just 1.4 times coverage of the offering size. By the standards of large corporate bond sales, the ratio signals notably muted enthusiasm. High-grade issuances of this scale typically draw far stronger oversubscription, with order books often reaching two to three times the deal size or more.

The proceeds are earmarked for stock buybacks, a use of funds that has drawn scrutiny from fixed-income investors wary of a company leveraging its balance sheet to return capital to equity holders rather than investing in growth or reducing existing obligations. Salesforce, the San Francisco-based enterprise software maker best known for its customer relationship management platform, is effectively asking bondholders to fund a transfer of value to shareholders — a proposition that becomes less attractive when questions loom about a company's long-term competitive positioning.

Those questions center squarely on artificial intelligence. A broader selloff in software stocks has reflected growing investor anxiety that AI-native tools could erode the dominance of established enterprise platforms. Salesforce, which generates revenue by helping corporations track and manage client relationships, faces a market increasingly skeptical that legacy software providers can maintain pricing power and market share as AI reshapes how businesses operate. The lukewarm bond reception arrives against that backdrop of uncertainty, suggesting debt investors share equity investors' apprehension.

The deal nonetheless ranks among the largest U.S. investment-grade corporate bond offerings in recent memory. Its sheer size — $25 billion across multiple tranches — tested the market's absorptive capacity at a moment when risk sentiment toward the technology sector remains fragile. While Salesforce ultimately placed the full offering, the thin oversubscription may signal that the company paid a premium in yield to clear the market, raising its cost of capital at a time when demonstrating financial discipline matters most to investors.

articles.headline: Replit Triples Valuation to $9 Billion in Just Six Months
articles.subheadline: The vibe-coding platform raised $400 million in a Series D as it eyes $1 billion in annual recurring revenue by year's end
articles.body:
Replit, the AI-powered coding platform that has become synonymous with the "vibe coding" movement, announced Wednesday that it has raised $400 million in a Series D round at a $9 billion valuation — tripling the $3 billion mark it reached just six months ago.

Georgian Partners, a returning investor, led the round, with participation from a roster of heavyweight backers including G Squared, Prysm Capital, Coatue, Andreessen Horowitz, Craft Ventures, Y Combinator, Accenture Ventures, Okta Ventures, and Databricks Ventures. CEO Amjad Masad noted on X that celebrity angel investors Shaquille O'Neal and Jared Leto also joined the round. The funding follows a $250 million raise in September 2025, when the company reported it was tracking toward $150 million in annualized revenue.

Replit declined to share updated revenue figures but told Forbes it aims to reach $1 billion in annual recurring revenue by the end of 2026 — a target that, if achieved, would represent roughly a sevenfold increase from its September run rate. That kind of growth trajectory would place Replit among the fastest-scaling enterprise software companies in recent memory, though the gap between aspiration and execution remains significant.

The company's surge reflects the explosive demand for tools that allow non-programmers to build software using natural language prompts — a category broadly dubbed vibe coding. Replit's platform lets users describe what they want in plain English, with AI handling the actual code generation. The approach has resonated far beyond the traditional developer community, opening up software creation to product managers, entrepreneurs, and hobbyists. Masad has previously acknowledged that the company spent roughly nine years building its technology before a controversial pivot away from professional developers toward non-technical users unlocked its current momentum.

The round lands amid a broader venture capital frenzy around AI-native startups. Nearly 40 new unicorns have been minted so far in 2026, according to TechCrunch data, with AI companies commanding an outsized share of funding. Replit's valuation jump — from $3 billion to $9 billion in half a year — underscores how quickly investors are repricing companies at the center of the generative AI wave, even as questions persist about whether current revenue multiples can be sustained.

articles.headline: Stocks Stall as Oil Surges Past $87 Amid Iran War Fears
articles.subheadline: Largest-ever IEA emergency stockpile release fails to cool crude rally; Treasury yields climb as rate-cut hopes fade
articles.body:
U.S. equities struggled to find direction on Tuesday as escalating geopolitical tensions tied to the conflict in Iran overshadowed a relatively benign inflation report, while oil prices continued their relentless climb despite an unprecedented intervention by the world's wealthiest nations.

West Texas Intermediate crude surged past $87 a barrel even after the International Energy Agency approved its largest-ever release of emergency petroleum stockpiles — a coordinated move by member nations that markets quickly dismissed as a temporary stopgap rather than a lasting solution. The rally in energy prices has injected fresh uncertainty into an already fragile economic outlook, raising the specter of an oil-driven inflation shock that could complicate the Federal Reserve's policy path.

The S&P 500 wavered throughout the session as traders toggled between geopolitical headlines and economic data. Inflation figures released Tuesday, which reflected conditions before the outbreak of the Iran conflict, came in relatively tame — but did little to lift sentiment given the dramatic shift in the energy landscape since those numbers were collected. Bond markets also came under pressure, with Treasury prices sliding as investors recalibrated their expectations for monetary easing. Fed funds futures now imply just a single interest rate cut for the remainder of 2026, a sharp pullback from the more dovish trajectory markets had priced in before hostilities erupted.

The IEA's decision to deploy emergency reserves at a historic scale underscores the severity of the supply disruption, yet the market's muted response suggests traders see the release as buying time rather than resolving the underlying crisis. With Iranian supply effectively sidelined and no clear diplomatic off-ramp in sight, crude could face sustained upward pressure that ripples through consumer prices, corporate margins, and central bank calculus worldwide.

For the Federal Reserve, the collision of geopolitical risk and energy-driven inflation presents a particularly uncomfortable dilemma. Cutting rates to support growth risks stoking price pressures already being amplified by the oil shock, while holding firm could deepen the economic drag. Wall Street appears to be bracing for a prolonged period of uncertainty, with volatility likely to remain elevated as long as the conflict — and its consequences for global energy markets — remains unresolved.

articles.headline: Trump's Call for Iran Uprising Echoes a Dangerous 1991 Precedent
articles.subheadline: BBC's Jeremy Bowen draws stark parallels between Bush's fateful Iraq appeal and current US-Israeli rhetoric toward Tehran
articles.body:
As the United States and Israel prosecute an ongoing military campaign against Iran, calls from President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for Iranians to rise up and overthrow the Islamic Republic carry an ominous historical echo — one that ended in catastrophe for those who heeded a similar American appeal three decades ago.

BBC International Editor Jeremy Bowen, a veteran correspondent who covered the 1991 Gulf War from inside Baghdad, has drawn a direct line between the current rhetoric and President George H.W. Bush's February 15, 1991, speech at a Patriot missile factory in Massachusetts. In that address, Bush urged "the Iraqi military and the Iraqi people to take matters into their own hands and force Saddam Hussein, the dictator, to step aside." Iraqi Shias in the south and Kurds in the north took the American president at his word and launched armed revolts. The United States did not intervene. Saddam Hussein's forces, permitted to retain their helicopter gunships under ceasefire terms, launched a brutal counteroffensive that killed thousands. Bowen, who witnessed Kurdish families carrying the bodies of children who froze to death on mountainsides while fleeing the regime's reprisals, described the consequences as devastating and long-lasting.

The current conflict, which Bowen characterizes as a "third Gulf war" aimed at dismantling Iran's regional power — power that paradoxically grew after the 2003 U.S. invasion removed Tehran's chief rival, Saddam Hussein — is the first to be conducted as a joint U.S.-Israeli military venture. Netanyahu has described it as the fulfillment of a 40-year ambition to "smite the terror regime hip and thigh." Recent polling shows the war is unpopular among Americans, and it has alarmed traditional U.S. allies, particularly in Europe, where Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth — who has rebranded his title as "Secretary of War" — dismissed concerns about the lack of UN authorization as hand-wringing.

The stakes of the current campaign are immense but the endgame remains dangerously undefined. Proponents argue that dismantling Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure will make the world safer, pointing to the regime's violent crackdown that killed thousands of its own citizens in January and its uranium enrichment to near-weapons-grade levels. But critics warn that the 2003 Iraq invasion, which toppled a dictator without a viable plan for what came next, unleashed years of sectarian bloodshed, hundreds of thousands of deaths, and a power vacuum that gave rise to the Islamic State. Iran's response to the current strikes has already sought to spread the conflict, disrupt global energy markets, and fracture carefully cultivated U.S. alliances with Gulf states that had tried to prevent the war.

Bowen's analysis underscores a recurring pattern in the region: each Gulf war has planted the seeds of the next. Whether this conflict breaks that cycle or accelerates it may depend on whether Washington and Jerusalem have answers to the question that went unanswered in 1991 and again in 2003 — what happens the day after the regime falls.

articles.headline: S&P 500 Slips as Strait of Hormuz Tensions Overshadow Cooling CPI
articles.subheadline: Brent crude surges nearly 6% to $93 a barrel as investors weigh escalating Middle East conflict against easing inflation data
articles.body:
U.S. equities edged lower on Wednesday as the widening conflict in the Middle East and a sharp rally in oil prices overshadowed a consumer inflation report that might otherwise have cheered markets.

The S&P 500 fell 0.1% on March 11, marking a second consecutive session of declines, while the Nasdaq 100 finished roughly flat. The modest losses came even as the latest Consumer Price Index data — collected before the onset of hostilities — pointed to easing price pressures, a signal that under calmer geopolitical conditions would likely have buoyed risk assets. Instead, investors focused squarely on the threat of mine deployment near the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil shipments. President Donald Trump sought to play down the severity of the threat, but markets were unconvinced.

Brent crude surged 5.9% to $92.99 a barrel, reflecting growing fears that prolonged disruption in the Persian Gulf could constrain global energy supply and reignite inflationary pressures just as they appeared to be cooling. The jump in oil prices raises the uncomfortable possibility that the Federal Reserve's path toward rate cuts could be complicated by a supply-driven price shock — a scenario that would squeeze both consumers and corporate margins simultaneously.

Technical signals added to the cautious mood. The S&P 500 remains below both its 50-day and 100-day moving averages, levels closely watched by momentum traders as indicators of broader trend direction. Wall Street analysts are now scanning charts to assess potential support levels, with some warning that a sustained breach of these thresholds could invite further selling.

The session underscored a growing tension at the heart of the current market environment: fundamental economic data suggests the U.S. economy is on a disinflationary glide path, but geopolitical risk is threatening to overwhelm that narrative. With the Iran conflict showing no signs of de-escalation and oil prices climbing toward triple digits, investors face the prospect of a market increasingly driven by headlines from the Middle East rather than data out of Washington. How long that dynamic persists may depend on whether diplomatic channels can defuse the Hormuz situation before energy markets price in a worst-case scenario.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Decouples From Tech Stocks, Shows Rare Strength at $70K
articles.subheadline: Analysts cite seller exhaustion, a flipping gold correlation, and returning ETF inflows as signs of stabilization
articles.body:
Bitcoin is displaying unusual resilience near the $70,000 level this week, outperforming major equity indices and traditional safe havens even as geopolitical tensions surrounding the Iran conflict rattle global markets. The divergence is prompting analysts to ask whether the largest cryptocurrency may be entering a new phase after months of declines.

As of Wednesday, bitcoin traded just below $71,000, having rallied roughly 7% from its Sunday evening lows. Over the same span, the Nasdaq 100 and S&P 500 were essentially flat, and gold posted only modest gains. For the month of March, bitcoin is the sole gainer among the three asset classes — a notable reversal for a market that spent much of the past several months tracking software stocks almost tick for tick. BlackRock's spot bitcoin ETF (IBIT) has risen 3.75% over the past five trading days, while the iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) has declined 2.45%, underscoring the early decoupling.

Several structural signals are reinforcing the cautiously optimistic outlook. Aurelie Barthere, principal research analyst at blockchain analytics firm Nansen, pointed to bitcoin's muted reaction to fresh geopolitical headlines as evidence of seller exhaustion. "Bitcoin's downside sensitivity has been relatively limited," she said, noting that European benchmarks like the Euro Stoxx index have fallen more sharply during the same period of macro stress. Bryan Tan, a trader at crypto market-maker Wintermute, highlighted a shift in bitcoin's correlation with gold — flipping from -0.49 to +0.16 in just one week. Rather than acting as opposing risk trades, both assets have been rising in tandem as the U.S. dollar weakens. "If this correlation continues trending positively, it shifts the narrative around BTC in a conflict environment from 'sell the risk asset' to something more nuanced," Tan said.

Perhaps the most tangible catalyst is the return of institutional money through spot bitcoin ETFs. After hemorrhaging more than $3 billion in net outflows between November and February, U.S.-listed bitcoin ETFs have seen a sharp reversal in March, with IBIT alone attracting nearly $1 billion in fresh capital so far this month, according to data from SoSoValue. Joe Edwards, head of research at Enigma, called a sustained recovery in ETF demand "critical" for bitcoin's next leg higher, noting that many analysts view access to deeper institutional capital pools — particularly retail brokerage accounts holding ETF shares — as the key driver of future growth.

Not everyone is convinced the bottom is in. Bloomberg strategist Mike McGlone reiterated a bearish call this week that bitcoin could eventually fall below $10,000, citing a prolonged macro-driven unwind. Most industry analysts dismissed the scenario as requiring an extreme global liquidity crisis, but the existence of such calls underscores the uncertainty still hanging over digital asset markets. For now, however, the weight of evidence — diminishing sell pressure, improving fund flows, and a shifting relationship with traditional havens — suggests bitcoin may be finding firmer footing as it heads into the second quarter.

articles.headline: Foreign Hacker Breached FBI Systems, Accessed Epstein Case Files
articles.subheadline: A vulnerable server at the bureau's Child Exploitation Forensic Lab in New York exposed sensitive investigation materials in 2023
articles.body:
An unidentified foreign hacker infiltrated the FBI's New York field office in 2023 and gained access to files connected to the bureau's investigation of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, according to a Reuters report citing court documents and a source with knowledge of the breach.

The intrusion exploited a server at the Child Exploitation Forensic Lab housed within the FBI's New York field office — a server that had been inadvertently left exposed by a special agent working on the Epstein case. Court documents indicate the hacker's activities "included combing through certain files pertaining to the Epstein investigation," raising serious questions about the security of one of the most scrutinized criminal probes in recent American history.

In a remarkable detail, the hacker reportedly did not even realize they had broken into FBI systems until agents contacted them and arranged a video call, during which bureau personnel displayed their credentials to confirm the target's identity. The episode underscores both the severity of the vulnerability and the apparent opportunistic nature of the breach.

The FBI confirmed the incident in a statement, saying it had contained the affected network and determined the breach to be isolated. "The FBI restricted access to the malicious actor and rectified the network," a bureau spokesperson said, adding that the investigation remains ongoing. The agency did not disclose the hacker's nationality or the specific nature of the compromised materials.

The revelation arrives at a politically sensitive moment. The Epstein case — which involves allegations of sex trafficking and connections to numerous prominent figures — has remained a subject of intense public interest since the financier's death in a Manhattan jail cell in August 2019. Any compromise of investigative files raises concerns about the integrity of evidence, the exposure of victim identities, and possible intelligence implications if a foreign actor obtained sensitive law enforcement materials. Separately, Reuters noted that a hacker linked to the Epstein case was recently removed from the website of Black Hat, the prominent cybersecurity conference, though the connection between the two developments was not immediately clear.

articles.headline: Ripple Buyback Values Blockchain Firm at $50 Billion Amid Crypto Slump
articles.subheadline: The $750 million share repurchase implies a 25% valuation jump since November despite a broad market downturn
articles.body:
Ripple, the blockchain payments company behind the XRP Ledger network, has launched a $750 million share buyback program that would place the firm's implied valuation at approximately $50 billion, according to a Bloomberg report on Wednesday. The figure represents a striking 25% premium to the $40 billion valuation at which Ripple raised capital just four months ago — even as the broader crypto market has suffered significant losses in the interim.

The tender offer, open to both investors and employees, is expected to run through April. It follows a $500 million funding round in November that attracted heavyweight backers including affiliates of Fortress Investment Group and Citadel Securities, as well as crypto-native firms Pantera Capital, Galaxy Digital, Brevan Howard, and Marshall Wace. That Ripple can command a higher valuation now, with bitcoin and XRP both down 30% to 40% since that round, underscores the market's confidence in the company's rapidly expanding enterprise business.

Ripple has been on an aggressive acquisition spree, deploying billions to broaden its footprint in institutional digital-asset infrastructure. The company completed a $1.25 billion purchase of prime brokerage Hidden Road and spent another $1 billion to acquire corporate treasury platform GTreasury. It also operates a U.S. dollar stablecoin, RLUSD, which has reached $1.5 billion in circulation through the firm's custody division. Ripple says its payments ecosystem has processed more than $100 billion in transactions, serving banks and payment providers seeking faster cross-border settlement.

The buyback signals a level of financial confidence unusual for a private crypto company navigating bearish conditions. Share repurchases are typically associated with mature public corporations looking to return capital to shareholders, and executing one at a premium valuation during a downturn suggests Ripple's leadership views the current market sentiment as disconnected from the firm's fundamentals. CEO Brad Garlinghouse, who recently testified before the U.S. Senate, has positioned Ripple as a bridge between traditional finance and blockchain-based payments.

For the broader digital-asset industry, Ripple's valuation trajectory offers a counterpoint to the prevailing gloom. While token prices have retreated sharply from their highs, institutional infrastructure companies continue to attract capital and command premium multiples — a dynamic that may define the next phase of crypto market maturation.
